mappings.rationaleAI: Pi's irrationality and transcendence connect to number theory (E.2), its geometric definition to trigonometry (C.4.b), and its centrality in calculus to real analysis (D.1, D.1.c).
Gödel's incompleteness theorems demonstrate limitations of axiomatic set theory (A.3.b) and foundational axiomatic reasoning (A.3).
Number theory in cryptography (E.2), cubic equations in algebra (B.2), non-Euclidean geometry for relativity (C.2), probability from games of chance (D.6).
Imaginary numbers from cubic equations (B.2.d), complex number plane and complex analysis (D.2.a), complex coordinates in geometry (C.3.c).
Euclid's proof of infinite primes (E.2.a), Riemann zeta function connecting to analytic number theory (E.2.c).
Zero and decimal place-value (B.1.c), arithmetic definitions (B.1.a), Vedic altar geometry (C.1).
Cantor's transfinite cardinals (A.2.b), continuum hypothesis (A.3), Zeno's paradoxes and limits (D.1.a).
Probability from gambling (D.6.a) to formalization on finite dimensional spaces (D.6.b).
Zeno's paradoxes stimulated set theory (A.2) and real analysis (D.1.a).
Zero in arithmetic (B.1.a) and place-value notation (B.1.c).
Euclid's Elements: axiomatic geometry (C.1.a) and elementary number theory (E.2.a).
Euclidean geometry (C.1), Arabic algebra (B.2), analytic geometry (C.4), calculus (D.1.c).
Symmetry formalized through group theory (B.4.b).

mappings.episodeTitle: Maths in the Early Islamic World
mappings.rationaleAI: Al-Khwarizmi's algebra (B.2), cubic equations (B.2.d), trigonometry (C.4.b), combinatorics (E.1).
Poincaré conjecture in topology (F.1.a), Ricci flow (F.2.d), dynamical systems (D.3.d).
Newton and Leibniz: differential (D.1.c) and integral calculus (D.1.d).
Euler's number e in real analysis (D.1.b) and logarithms (B.1.e).
Erdős: number theory (E.2), graph theory (E.1.b), set theory (A), probabilistic methods (D.6).
Pythagorean theorem in geometry (C.1) and number theory (E.2.a).
Gauss: number theory (E.2.a), geometric constructions (C.1.d), non-Euclidean geometry (C.2).
Fermat's Last Theorem (E.2.a), Wiles's proof via algebraic number theory (E.2.b).
Noether's foundational contributions to abstract algebra, particularly to linear and multilinear algebra and the theory of algebraic structures (B.3), transformed the discipline.
The episode discusses how mathematics underpins physics, biology, and neuroscience, engaging the branches of mathematics broadly.
The discussion of symmetry, recursion, and combinatorics as frameworks for analyzing narratives engages branches of mathematics.
